item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here is a nitpick: The chemicals come in little glass bottles, and the first chemical listed is hydrofluoric acid. This is such an especially nasty acid, that it eats through glass (that's what it's used for, really). So is there an issue here, or can it be handwaved between Dr Wondertainment, anomalous item, or maybe HF came in a plastic bottle but they neglected to mention that in the description?

(Etiquette question: Should this post and the one I made above have been a single post?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well written, it is the right length, no problems on its own… However, it's not on its own; there are all the other Dr Wondertainment items. The problem is: The moment I saw the article name, without opening the article, I knew exactly what I was going to get. I knew it was going to be Dr Wondertainment, and I knew what it was going to be. I even expected the reordering stuff. That's why I'm not upvoting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Little nitpick that really bothers me, the '!' Should really come before the TM.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Yes

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

For a Dr. Wondertainment article, this one really does capture that Dr. W feel of mad science, whimsy, and fridge horror. That aside, the DIY chemistry/science aspect reminded me strongly of SCP-2702 and it doesn't seem to far out of character for Dr. Wondertainment to advertise for a like minded individual, so perhaps there is a small ad for "Professor Abnormal's Science Lab" with the ads for the other science kits. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The back page of each booklet contains a re-order form for the chemical compounds. Re-order requests have consistently been filled within 4-6 weeks, although the supplied mailing address does not exist. At the bottom of the re-order form is an advertisement to purchase "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Physicist Kit™", "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Geologist Kit™!", and "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Geneticist Kit™!". Attempts to order these kits using the re-order form have consistently resulted in form letters stating that the kits are available in retail outlets only.

To this:

The back page of each booklet contains a re-order form for the chemical compounds. Re-order requests have consistently been filled within 4-6 weeks, although the supplied mailing address does not exist. At the bottom of the re-order form is an advertisement to purchase "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Physicist Kit™", "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Geologist Kit™!", "Dr. Wondertainment's Super Science™ Li'l Geneticist Kit™!", as well as an advertisement for a television program entitled "Professor Abnormal's Science Lab". Attempts to order these kits using the re-order form have consistently resulted in form letters stating that the kits are available in retail outlets only.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, since SCP-092 got a test log, do you guys think it would be cool if this got a small little tiny weenie test log too? ( and would Drewbear be ok with that? )

Edit : wrong SCP, it was the one with the sharp plant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, the Li'l Geneticist Kit was supposed to be too sci-fi for reality…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for ethnically diverse group of children

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a writing community, and most of the users here have college level experience or above. Please check your posts for spelling, grammar, and basic comprehensibility before posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too was verry disapointed that the vanishing cream only lasted 5 minuits. I read the directions halfway and when i half-apeared out of nowhere in the bank…

Yah, kinda embarrassing.

But that was when i was 7.

WARNING! The glow in the dark stuff is highly radioactive.

ment to be post above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea. It seems like something out of a kid's TV show, and the implications of application in real life are interesting. Plus, it's a Wondertaintmenttm skip that isn't horribly creepy! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like yours better than 1103, i want a cubic kilometer smoke bomb. upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Theally said. Oobleck is just a mixture of water and corn starch (I think it's 1:1 but I'm not sure) that's a non-Newtonian liquid. And yes, it was named after the Dr. Seuss substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Real life oobleck is just water and corn starch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oobleck is a simple compound? I'm staying neutral, but the Dr. Seuss reference isn't to blame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sodium hydroxide worries you? Liquid mercury? I played with both of when I was the listed age for this Dr. Wondertainment kit. I mean, when a thermometer broke an adult would let us play with the mercury.

Acute exposure to elemental mercury is not all that bad. It's chronic exposure to elemental mercury, as well as the organometallics that you really want to avoid. Oh, and you'd do best not to inhale elemental mercury vapor. It's not lethal, but from what I hear it's not pleasant.

Sodium hydroxide is bog standard. Lot's of homes keep quite strong alkaline solutions under the kitchen sink.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, those are the things you get when you follow the formula…

…and you can order more of the "magic ingredients"…

…and these are Foundation scientists who tend to throw science at the wall to see what sticks…

I'm thinking these go to a SPECIAL lab and they start mixing to "see what happens"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The previous Dr. Wondertainment thing I no-voted because it was creative but there was no scary/funny/interesting hook. This one…. I'm sorry, downvote. There's nothing that interests me going on here. It could be an addition to that "additional experiments" page possible (I may not be remembering the name correctly; sorry, I'm new!) but… I don't know. There's not much substance here for me to grab on. Make the kit do something more interesting?

Next day edit: I'm such an asshole when I drunk-post… Oh well! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh dear, and now I do.

That's why I'm a linguist and not a chemist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That shows you don't know very much about hydrofluoric acid (or, more likely, are confusing it with hydro chloric acid).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think yellowcake is a little more freaky. I mean, those are all things you'd use in high school chemistry, dangerous or not. But uranium? Not so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this is just the chemistry set! Imagine what the physics set is like!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It improves on the real one you could get in 1951:

http://www.orau.org/ptp/collection/atomictoys/GilbertU238Lab.htm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pooped myself at the mention of "Lil' Geneticist". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And yet, no matter the ingredients, so long as you follow the instructions, the final result will be harmless and non-toxic. Dr. Wondertainment isn't responsible for any injuries or accidents that may occur if you misuse the ingredients or don't follow the instructions, however. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a quick overview of the chemicals included could be combined (especially in the hands of someone with "SUPER ___" stuff) into anything from nerve agents to mutagenic compounds to low-yield (or maybe NOT so low-yield) nuclear weaponry… Or unlimited free energy forever. Or a hole dug to the center of the god damn planet, and only stop then because that's the gravitational center.

Yeah, this thing needs containment. Also, a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only the yellowcake freaked you out?!

This thing includes hydrofluoric acid, colloidal silver, sodium hydroxide, and liquid mercury!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you may have been outclassed, which is a shame because this is good. I got a little creep factor from the ordering notes near the end, and I did a double-take at the yellowcake hey, that rhymes!.

My question now is, what happens when you, being a scientist in the employ of a secret organization dedicated to finding, containing and running tests on anomalous things, apply some of those extra chemical compounds to other stuff? Work outside the instructions, in other words. (With adult supervision, of course.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dangerous? What are you talking about dangerous? It clearly states that it should only be done with adult supervision, and following the instructions! There's no danger in that! :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't want this to become a "thing wat maeks moar SCPs!!", so I kept the time limit below the threshold of significant usefullness. Same thing for the other "special" items; these are the kinds of things that old-time chemistry sets taught kids how to make, or at least gave them the skills to make.

And it actually is true that some (much) older chemistry sets used to include radioactive elements, back before the long-term health effects became widely known. It's a miracle that our grandparents managed to survive to adulthood. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm disappointed the 'vanishing cream' only lasts 5 minutes. It'd make an awesome effect if you used it in combination with body paint for a hallowe'en costume. But I'm weird like that.

I had a chemistry set as a kid, but it wasn't nearly as cool as that. I would have used it to make my brothers vanish - permanently. So it's probably best that it wasn't around 35 years ago.

I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neato!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smoke bombs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said, I like.

Still think you should have made it Young Chemist :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly, this is one of Dr. Wondertainment's least anomalous items.

Still pretty dangerous though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I would like to point out that the glow in the dark paint glows about half the brightness of the sun (brighter than normal artificial lighting) and the smoke pellets produce enough smoke to fill a 1 km x 1 km x 1km cube. Not sure yet whether or not I'm going to add anything, but vote as you feel appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah… this one just doesn't do it for me. In my opinion glow in the dark paint and smoke pellets aren't really wondertainment-y. I'll give you some time to fix it before casting my vote, but as it stand I'm going to -1 it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't interest me at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I kinda wanna write one of the three Lil Scientist kits you posted… :|

But then not really, cuz it'd be just another lil misters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting factoid: I actually wrote this simultaneously as VAElynx wrote SCP-1103. He just managed to get his posted first while I was at work. :P I think they're sufficiently different, though.
